description: Public database of quantum-vulnerable cryptographic patterns in EVM smart contracts. CVE-equivalent for post-quantum risk in Solidity/EVM. Maintained by QuantumScan (quantumscan.io).

patterns.name: ecrecover — secp256k1 ECDSA signature verification
patterns.description: ecrecover is the EVM precompile (address 0x01) that implements secp256k1 ECDSA signature recovery. All Ethereum account ownership verification uses this primitive. A cryptographically-relevant quantum computer running Shor's algorithm can recover the private key from a public key in polynomial time, breaking all ecrecover-based authorization.
affected_protocols: Any contract using msg.sender from EOA
patterns.migration_target: ML-DSA-65 (NIST FIPS 204) via future EVM precompile. Interim: account abstraction (ERC-4337) with PQC validator contract.

patterns.name: EIP-712 typed data signatures — secp256k1 domain-separated ECDSA
patterns.description: EIP-712 adds a domain separator to prevent cross-contract/cross-chain replay attacks, but the underlying signature scheme is still secp256k1 ECDSA. All EIP-712-signed messages (permit, delegation, votes, auction bids) are quantum-vulnerable.
patterns.hndl_note: Messages signed off-chain and relayed can be harvested today and the signing key recovered post-Q-Day.
patterns.migration_target: ML-DSA-65 (NIST FIPS 204). Requires new EIP-712-equivalent standard with ML-DSA domain encoding.
references: EIP-712 — Typed structured data hashing and signing

patterns.name: ERC-2612 permit() — gasless approval via ECDSA
patterns.affected_opcode: CALL to precompile 0x01 (via ecrecover inside permit())
patterns.description: ERC-2612 enables gasless ERC-20 approvals through off-chain ECDSA signatures. The permit() function calls ecrecover internally. Because the signature is created off-chain, it is particularly exposed to HNDL: an adversary can harvest the signed permit message today and, after Q-Day, forge the owner's signature to drain allowances.
patterns.hndl_note: Signed permit messages broadcast on-chain are permanently visible. Key recovery post-Q-Day enables replay/drain.
patterns.migration_target: No PQC permit standard exists yet. Options: (1) return to on-chain approve() + multicall, (2) ERC-4337 PQC validator, (3) wait for EIP-7789 + ML-DSA permit standard.
references: EIP-2612 — Permit Extension for EIP-20 Signed Approvals

patterns.name: Gnosis Safe / Safe{Wallet} N-of-M ECDSA multisig
patterns.description: Gnosis Safe verifies N-of-M ECDSA signatures from owner accounts. Each owner key is a secp256k1 key pair. Even a 5-of-7 Safe is quantum-vulnerable: a quantum computer recovers any single signer's private key, trivially defeats the M-of-N threshold if M=1 owners are sufficient, and forces the entire multisig to be replaced.
patterns.migration_target: Safe{Wallet} PQC module (in development). Each signer migrates to ML-DSA-65 key pair. ERC-4337 Safe module enables quantum-safe validation.
references: EIP-1271 — Standard Signature Validation Method for Contracts

patterns.name: Chainlink Oracle ECDSA DON — secp256k1 threshold signatures
patterns.description: Chainlink's Decentralized Oracle Network (DON) uses threshold ECDSA (secp256k1) for off-chain report signing. While the on-chain contract doesn't call ecrecover directly, the data it consumes is authenticated by quantum-vulnerable ECDSA. A quantum attacker could forge oracle price reports by recovering DON node keys — causing DeFi protocols relying on Chainlink to use malicious prices.
patterns.hndl_note: DON nodes send signed reports via long-lived TLS sessions. HNDL attack on those sessions could expose signing keys before Q-Day.
affected_protocols: All Chainlink price feeds consumers (Aave, Compound, GMX, Synthetix)
patterns.migration_target: Monitor Chainlink PQC roadmap. Chainlink hasn't published a timeline. Interim: use multi-oracle aggregation (Chainlink + Pyth + Chronicle) to reduce single-oracle failure risk.

patterns.name: ERC-4337 UserOperation ECDSA validation
patterns.description: ERC-4337 Account Abstraction introduces a flexible signature validation hook (validateUserOp). Most current implementations use ECDSA/secp256k1 — the default SimpleAccount uses ecrecover. This means AA wallets are quantum-vulnerable at the validation layer unless a custom PQC validator is deployed.
patterns.migration_target: ERC-4337 is the MIGRATION PATH for PQC: replace ECDSA validator with ML-DSA validator contract. This is the recommended upgrade route for all EOAs post-Q-Day.
references: EIP-4337 — Account Abstraction Using Alt Mempool
Vitalik Buterin — ERC-4337 as PQC migration path (2024)

patterns.name: Uniswap Permit2 / position signature — ECDSA batch approval
patterns.description: Uniswap's Permit2 contract (0x000000000022D473030F116dDEE9F6B43aC78BA3) enables unified batch token approvals via EIP-712 ECDSA signatures. It is deployed on 30+ chains and used by Uniswap v3/v4, 1inch, Matcha, and dozens of aggregators. All Permit2 signatures are secp256k1 ECDSA — quantum-vulnerable.
patterns.hndl_note: Permit2 messages are broadcast publicly. Harvested today, key recovery post-Q-Day allows draining all approved tokens.
patterns.migration_target: Requires Permit2 v2 with ML-DSA support. No timeline published by Uniswap Labs.

patterns.name: ZK-ECDSA circuits — ECDSA verification inside Groth16/PLONK proofs
patterns.description: Several ZK protocols prove ECDSA signature knowledge inside a ZK circuit (e.g., zkEmail, Noir ECDSA, circom-ecdsa). The ZK proof itself doesn't reveal the private key, but the underlying signature scheme is secp256k1 ECDSA. Post-Q-Day, the signer's key can be recovered from any historically-published signature, making the 'secret' in the ZK proof retroactively exposed.
patterns.hndl_note: The original ECDSA signature that the ZK proof attests to must have been broadcast somewhere. HNDL captures that original signature.
patterns.migration_target: ZK circuits proving ML-DSA knowledge (lattice-based). Research prototype: STARK-based ML-DSA circuits exist. No production deployment yet.

patterns.name: ERC-1271 isValidSignature — contract-based ECDSA delegation
patterns.description: ERC-1271 allows contracts to validate signatures on behalf of smart accounts and DAOs. While the interface is algorithm-agnostic, virtually all current implementations delegate to ECDSA/ecrecover. This is also the interface used by Safe{Wallet} and Gnosis Safe.
patterns.migration_target: ERC-1271 is algorithm-agnostic. Upgrade the implementation to use ML-DSA-65 internally. No change to the interface required.
references: EIP-1271 — Standard Signature Validation Method for Contracts

patterns.name: Bridge / Sequencer / Relayer ECDSA — HNDL exposure via long-lived TLS sessions
patterns.description: Bridge relayers, L2 sequencers, and oracle relay nodes sign messages off-chain using secp256k1 ECDSA before submitting them on-chain. These nodes maintain long-lived TLS sessions (hours/days) with peer nodes. Harvest Now, Decrypt Later (HNDL) attacks target these sessions: a quantum adversary captures the TLS-encrypted traffic today and, after Q-Day, decrypts it to extract signing keys or the messages themselves. This enables retroactive forgery of bridge attestations.
patterns.hndl_note: HNDL is the most imminent quantum threat to bridges — it doesn't require a full cryptographically-relevant quantum computer. Even partial decryption of TLS sessions may leak nonces or key material.
patterns.quantumscan_endpoint: POST /api/scan/relayer — HNDL Risk Score 0–100 for relayer endpoints
patterns.migration_target: Hybrid PQC TLS: X25519Kyber768 (NIST FIPS 203 ML-KEM hybrid KEX) for transport. Signing: ML-DSA-65 for on-chain-submitted messages. TLS 1.3 minimum with PQC cipher suite.
references: NIST SP 800-227 (draft) — Guidelines for PQC Migration
ETSI GR QSC 006 — HNDL threat model
NSA CNSA 2.0 — deadline 2030 for critical infrastructure

patterns.name: BLS12-381 pairing curve — Ethereum PoS validator signatures
patterns.description: Ethereum PoS uses BLS12-381 for validator signatures. BLS pairing curves are believed to be quantum-vulnerable (Shor's algorithm attacks discrete log on the base field), though no efficient full attack exists yet. EIP-2537 exposes BLS12-381 precompiles to smart contracts. Contracts using these precompiles for verification inherit the underlying quantum risk.
patterns.migration_target: No NIST PQC standard for pairings yet. Ethereum Foundation PQC roadmap includes EIP-7549 (aggregate attestation format). Monitor IETF PQUIP group.
references: EIP-2537 — Precompile for BLS12-381 curve operations

patterns.name: LayerZero / Wormhole cross-chain ECDSA signature relay
patterns.affected_opcode: CALL to precompile 0x01 (via ecrecover in verifyVAA)
patterns.description: LayerZero DVN relayers and Wormhole guardians authenticate cross-chain messages with ECDSA threshold signatures. Compromising a single guardian key (or enough for threshold) via quantum key recovery allows an adversary to forge arbitrary cross-chain messages — enabling unauthorized token bridge withdrawals.
patterns.hndl_note: Guardian nodes use TLS-encrypted P2P channels. HNDL on those channels could leak signing keys before Q-Day.
affected_protocols: All protocols bridging via these networks (e.g., USDC CCTP, Pyth)
patterns.migration_target: ML-DSA-65 threshold signature scheme for guardian/DVN signing. Requires protocol upgrade.

patterns.name: assembly-level ecrecover precompile call (0x01)
patterns.description: Some gas-optimized contracts call the ecrecover precompile directly via inline assembly to avoid Solidity's memory overhead. The quantum vulnerability is identical to the high-level ecrecover(), but the pattern is harder for static analyzers to detect.
patterns.migration_target: Same as EVM-PQC-001. Assembly-level call does not change the underlying cryptographic vulnerability.

patterns.name: ecrecover with raw abi.encodePacked — signature replay vulnerability
patterns.description: Using ecrecover with abi.encodePacked (without a proper EIP-712 domain separator) creates both a quantum vulnerability AND a same-chain/cross-chain signature replay attack vector. This is a double vulnerability: the secp256k1 signature is Shor-vulnerable, and the lack of domain separator allows replay across contracts and chains. This pattern is the most dangerous combination in the database.
patterns.migration_target: Immediate: add EIP-712 domain separator. Long-term: ML-DSA-65 (NIST FIPS 204).

glossary.SHOR: Shor's algorithm breaks integer factorization (RSA) and discrete logarithm (ECDSA/DH) in polynomial time on a cryptographically-relevant quantum computer (CRQC).
glossary.GROVER: Grover's algorithm provides quadratic speedup for brute-force search, halving symmetric key security (AES-128 → 64-bit effective).
glossary.HNDL: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later — adversary captures encrypted traffic today and stores it for decryption after a CRQC is available.
glossary.CRQC: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer — a fault-tolerant quantum computer with enough logical qubits to run Shor's algorithm against production key sizes.
glossary.ML-DSA: Module Lattice Digital Signature Algorithm — NIST FIPS 204 (2024). Quantum-safe replacement for ECDSA/RSA signatures.
glossary.ML-KEM: Module Lattice Key Encapsulation Mechanism — NIST FIPS 203 (2024). Quantum-safe replacement for ECDH/RSA key exchange.
glossary.SLH-DSA: Stateless Hash-based Digital Signature Algorithm — NIST FIPS 205 (2024). Hash-based quantum-safe signature (conservative choice).
